2: he hemera kyriou hos kleptes en nykti houtōs erchetai: 'the day of the Lord comes like a thief in the night.' The 'Day of the Lord' (hemera kyriou) is Paul's use of the prophetic Day-of-YHWH tradition (Amos 5:18-20; Isaiah 2:12-22; Joel 2:11; Zephaniah 1:14-18) — originally a day of divine judgment. The 'thief in the night' image for its unexpectedness appears also in Jesus's eschatological teaching (Matthew 24:43-44; Luke 12:39-40) and later NT texts (2 Peter 3:10; Revelation 3:3; 1 Thessalonians 16:15), indicating a shared tradition. Paul applies it to the parousia: its timing is unknown, its arrival will be sudden, and preparedness (not calculation of dates) is the appropriate response.
1: "Times and dates" (chronōn kai tōn kairōn) — the dual terminology: chronos (chronological time, duration) and kairos (appointed time, the critical moment). The same pairing in Acts 1:7. The combination covers both duration and the decisive character of the end-time period.
3: "Destruction will come on them suddenly" (aiphnidios autois ephistatai olethros) — aiphnidios (sudden, unforeseen) + ephistatai (present: stands upon, is approaching). The destruction is already approaching before it arrives. "Labor pains on a pregnant woman" — the OT prophetic image for sudden, unavoidable, intensifying judgment (Isaiah 13:8; 1 Thessalonians 26:17; Jeremiah 4:31; 1 Thessalonians 6:24). Once begun, birth pains cannot be stopped or reversed.
4: "Not in darkness so that this day should surprise you like a thief" — en skotei (in darkness): not merely ignorance but the moral-eschatological state of those alienated from God. Being not-in-darkness means the Day's arrival will not be an ambush. The community's eschatological situation is different in kind from the world around it.
5: "Children of the light and children of the day" (huioi phōtos... huioi hēmeras) — "sons of" is a Semitic idiom for belonging to a category or being characterized by it. The dualistic light/darkness, day/night language echoes the Community Rule at Qumran (1QS 1 Thessalonians 1:9-10), though Paul's use is not deterministic. What matters is behaving consistently with one's ontological status.
6: "Let us not be like others, who are asleep" — the sleep/wakefulness metaphor now operates on two levels simultaneously: literal night-sleep (1 Thessalonians 5:7) and spiritual/moral somnolence. The play on koimōmenoi (sleeping in death, 1 Thessalonians 4) and katheudōsin (spiritually unaware, 1 Thessalonians 5) is deliberate and productive.
7: "Those who sleep, sleep at night, and those who get drunk, get drunk at night" — the observation functions as a social-cosmological axiom: night is the domain of sleep and intoxication. Day-people who conduct themselves as night-people have confused their ontological status. The logic is: be what you are; live according to the light to which you belong.
8: "Breastplate of faith and love... helmet of hope of salvation" (endysamenoi thōraka pisteōs kai agapēs kai perikephalian elpida sōtērias) — Paul adapts the divine warrior's armor from Isaiah 59:17 (where God wears righteousness as a breastplate and salvation as a helmet) to the Christian community. The three theological virtues reappear: faith-love form the breastplate (protecting the vital center); hope-of-salvation is the helmet (protecting the mind against despair).
9: "God did not appoint us to suffer wrath but to receive salvation through our Lord Jesus Christ" — etheto hēmas: divine intentionality behind the community's destiny. The contrast is absolute: orgē (eschatological judgment-wrath) vs peripoiēsin sōtērias (acquisition/obtaining of salvation). The community is destined for salvation; the wrath is real but is not their destination.
10: "Whether we are awake or asleep" — picking up the sleep/wake imagery but now meaning alive or dead (the same ambiguity as 1 Thessalonians 4:13-15). The constant regardless of condition is union with Christ: "we may live together with him" (hama syn autō zōmen). The parousia-hope is not primarily about events but about eternal communion with the Lord.
11: "Build each other up" (oikodomeite heis ton hena) — oikodomeō (to build a house/edifice) is Paul's architectural metaphor for community formation, dominant in 1 Cor 14. "Just as in fact you are doing" — the indicative before the imperative: you already do this; continue and intensify. Paul affirms existing practice before calling for more.
12: Three participles describing community leaders without formal titles: kopiontas (laboring exhaustingly — the same word as Paul's own toil in 1 Thessalonians 2:9), proistamenous (being set over, leading/caring — the leadership-root that appears in Romans 12:8; 1 Timothy 5:17), nouthetountas (warning, putting in mind — nous + tithēmi). Functional descriptions consistent with 1 Thessalonians' early date before institutionalized church offices.
13: "Hold them in the highest regard" (hēgeisthai en agapē huperekperissou) — huperekperissou is a triple compound: hyper (above) + ek (out of) + perissos (exceeding) = superabundantly-beyond-measure. One of Paul's characteristic push-past-the-limit compounds. The ground is "their work" (dia to ergon autōn) — the labor itself justifies the high regard, not merely their position or status.
14: Four imperatives for community practice: "warn the idle" (noutheteite tous ataktous — ataktos = disorderly, out of rank; a military term for soldiers out of formation; likely those who abandoned work because of eschatological excitement), "encourage the disheartened" (paramytheisthe tous oligopsychous — small-spirited, faint-hearted), "help the weak" (antechesthe tōn asthenōn — hold fast to/support the weak), "be patient with everyone" (makrothymeite pros pantas — long-tempered toward all, without exception).
15: "Make sure nobody pays back wrong for wrong" (mē apodidontes kakon anti kakou) — anti = in place of, in exchange for; the retaliation-logic of equivalence. "Always strive to do what is good" (to agathon diōkete) — diōkō = pursue, chase after; the same verb used for persecution. Good is to be pursued with the intensity that persecutors pursue harm.
16: "Rejoice always" (pantote chairete) — imperative mood makes joy a command. Pantote allows no exception. The brevity (two words in Greek) is itself significant — no qualification, no condition. Paul will elaborate (Philippians 4:4) but here the command is bare and unqualified.
17: "Pray continually" (adialeiptōs proseuchesthe) — adialeiptos = without interruption, without let-up; the same adverb as in 1 Thessalonians 1:2-3. Not a command for unbroken verbal prayer but for a prayerful orientation of the whole life — a disposition of continual address to God that underlies all activity.
18: "Give thanks in all circumstances" (en panti eucharistite) — en panti = in everything (not for everything, which would make suffering itself the object of thanks, but in every circumstance). "This is God's will for you in Christ Jesus" — the divine will specified here is the three-part triad of joy, prayer, thanksgiving; not primarily a set of rules but a posture of relationship.
19: "Do not quench the Spirit" (to pneuma mē sbennyte) — sbennymi (to extinguish fire) applied to the Spirit; the Spirit-as-fire is standard imagery (Matthew 3:11; Acts 2:3-4). The verb acknowledges that the community has the capacity to suppress the Spirit's activity — quenching implies neglecting or suppressing, particularly prophetic speech (1 Thessalonians 5:20).
20: "Do not treat prophecies with contempt" (prophēteias mē exoutheneite) — exoutheneō = to treat as nothing, nullify. The connection with 1 Thessalonians 5:19 (Spirit) and 1 Thessalonians 5:20 (prophecy) suggests that quenching the Spirit and despising prophecy are related acts. The Spirit's primary public expression is prophetic speech; suppressing it suppresses the Spirit.
21: "Test them all; hold on to what is good" (panta dokimazete, to kalon katechete) — dokimazō (test and if genuine, approve — the same word as 2:4 for God's testing of Paul). Prophecy is not to be uncritically received; the community bears discernment responsibility. Katechō (hold fast) = secure hold on the tested-and-approved good.
22: "Reject every kind of evil" (apo pantos eidous ponērou apechesthe) — eidos = form, kind, species: every species/form of evil, not merely obvious manifestations. Apechō (hold away from, abstain from) is an active distancing; the verb appears in contract papyri meaning "to receive in full and keep at a distance" — the transactional note is absent here but the active withdrawal remains.
23:
"Whole spirit, soul and body" (to pneuma kai hē psychē kai to sōma) — unique in Paul (elsewhere body/spirit or body/soul). This tripartite formula is probably a totality expression rather than a technical trichotomous anthropology: Paul is praying for comprehensive sanctification of the whole person, not defining the parts of the human with philosophical precision. The emphasis is on "through and through" (holoteleis — entirely complete) and "whole" (holoklēron — entire, without missing part).

"Blameless at the coming (parousia) of our Lord Jesus Christ" — the sanctification prayer is eschatologically oriented: completeness at the parousia is the horizon. The sanctification is God's work (1 Thessalonians 5:23a) grounded in God's faithfulness (1 Thessalonians 5:24).
24: "The one who calls you is faithful, and he will do it" (pistos ho kalōn humas, hos kai poiēsei) — pistos (faithful) as a divine attribute grounds the eschatological confidence. "He will do it" — future active indicative; unqualified. The God who calls is the same God who completes; calling and completing are both acts of the same faithful character.
25: "Brothers and sisters, pray for us" (adelphoi, proseuchesthe kai peri hēmōn) — Paul who has been praying for the Thessalonians (1 Thessalonians 1:2; 1 Thessalonians 3:10-13) requests their prayer in return. Apostolic and communal prayer is mutually constitutive; the apostle is not above the need for the community's intercession.
26: "Greet all God's people with a holy kiss" (aspasasthe tous adelphous pantas en philēmati hagiō) — the philēma hagion (holy kiss) appears in all four letters containing greetings (Romans 16:16; 1 Corinthians 16:20; 2 Corinthians 13:12). It was a standard greeting in Jewish and early Christian assemblies, physically enacting community belonging; "holy" distinguishes it from ordinary social greeting by its covenantal context.
27: "I charge you before the Lord to have this letter read to all" (enorkizō hymas ton kyrion) — enorkizō (to put under oath, adjure): a solemn charge by appeal to the Lord himself. The command for public reading to all members ensures the letter's authority is not limited to the literate or the leaders. This is one of the earliest explicit instructions for what became the practice of public scripture-reading in Christian assemblies.
28: "The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with you" — the closing benediction echoes and expands the opening salutation (1 Thessalonians 1:1: "Grace and peace to you from God and the Lord Jesus Christ"). The letter that began with grace-and-peace ends with grace alone; the single-term benediction sends the assembly forward in what the letter began with.
